content.problemStatement:
You are given a N×MN \times MN×M matrix AAA, filled with **distinct** numbers. You can start at any cell. If you are currently at cell (x1,y1)(x_1, y_1)(x1​,y1​), then you can jump to any other cell (x2,y2)(x_2, y_2)(x2​,y2​), if and only if the following two conditions are satisfied:



Ax2,y2>Ax1,y1A_{x_2, y_2} \gt A_{x_1, y_1}Ax2​,y2​​>Ax1​,y1​​




Manhattan distance between Ax2,y2A_{x_2, y_2}Ax2​,y2​​ and Ax1,y1A_{x_1, y_1}Ax1​,y1​​ >S \gt S>S. Formally, ∣x1−x2∣+∣y1−y2∣>S|x_1 - x_2| + |y_1 - y_2| \gt S∣x1​−x2​∣+∣y1​−y2​∣>S.



Let VVV be the set of the cells visited by you.

Maximize the value of ∑(x,y)∈V Ax,y\sum_{(x, y) \in V} \text{ }A_{x, y}∑(x,y)∈V​ Ax,y​.


Input Format

The first line of the input contains the number TTT - the number of test cases. Then the test cases follow.
The first line of each test case contains three integers N,M,SN, M, SN,M,S - the parameters defined in the statement.
Then NNN lines follow, with MMM integers in each line.
Each of the next NNN lines contains MMM integers, where the jjj-th integer in iii-th line is equal to Ai,jA_{i, j}Ai,j​


Output Format
For each testcase output the maximum possible value of ∑(x,y)∈V Ax,y\sum_{(x, y) \in V} \text{ }A_{x, y}∑(x,y)∈V​ Ax,y​.
constraints: It's guaranteed that all Ai,jA_{i, j}Ai,j​ are distinct.
It's guaranteed that sum of N×MN \times MN×M over all test cases doesn't exceed 10610^6106.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case-1: It is optimal start at cell (1,1)(1, 1)(1,1), then jump to cell (3,2)(3, 2)(3,2). Therefore answer is A1,1+A3,2=1+8=9A_{1, 1} + A_{3, 2} = 1 + 8 = 9A1,1​+A3,2​=1+8=9.
Test case-2: It is optimal to start at (2,2)(2, 2)(2,2) and follow this path: (2,2)→(2,1)→(1,2)→(1,1)(2, 2) \rightarrow (2, 1) \rightarrow (1, 2) \rightarrow (1, 1)(2,2)→(2,1)→(1,2)→(1,1). Therefore answer is the sum of all the four cells =394= 394=394.
